title: Debunking Cholesterol Myths: New Study Reveals Surprising Findings! With Dave Feldman

chunks.title: Introduction to LDL Study
chunks.summary: The podcast begins with an introduction to a new study concerning LDL levels and cardiovascular disease, led by Dave Feldman and Dr. Matthew Budoff. The results challenge traditional views on the correlation between high LDL levels and plaque formation in healthy individuals.
chunks.transcript: there's just no correlation between uh detected LDL levels at Baseline and resulting plaque on this week's podcast I had the pleasure of talking to my friend Dave Feldman Dave recently released a study that was done with Dr Matthew budoff a well-known cardiac radiologist at the Lun quiz Institute connected with UCLA looking at the progression of heart disease atherosclerosis in a cohort of people with elevated LDL and comparing that to a cohort of healthy indiv ual who didn't have elevated LDL let you guys listen to the podcast to hear about the results but I think they are striking the kind of things I've been talking about for a long time on this podcast contextual ideas around LDL and apob in relation to cardiovascular risk these findings are quite striking and I think that they raise a lot of important questions and I hope they are the beginning of a lot of important research for all of us so that we can truly understand how to best decrease our risk of cardiovascular disease moving forward enjoy the podcast

chunks.title: Lean Mass Hyperresponders
chunks.summary: Dave Feldman outlines the concept of lean mass hyperresponders, a group characterized by high LDL, high HDL, and low triglycerides. This segment discusses the rarity of these metrics in the general population and the significance of studying such individuals.
chunks.transcript: mean the first and most important thing to emphasize is this this study at the time that we were putting it together it's it's longitudinal it's on this phenotype that I know you've talked about that I've talked about all of us have talked about uh that I I designated in 2017 in a blog post it was a bit speculative at the time it's just that I kept it was pattern recognition I kept seeing this Triad of high LDL cholesterol High HDL cholesterol low triglycerides with a lot of people are metabolically healthy in the low carb space but in particular there seemed to be especially if somebody was very athletic but also very low carb and very lean they tended to have this Triad at a more extreme level to where HDL cholesterol would be very high along with very high LDL so this is the Triad of of lean mass hyporesponders it's it's an LDL of 200 or higher HDL of 80 or higher and triglycerides of 70 or lower and it's important to emphasize that those three things I just mentioned all three of them are rare in the general population you don't you don't typically come up with those for so for all three of them to be the same I kind of put it out there in 2017 just like called it that but I didn't actually know how many and it ended up exploding whole bunch of people came out of the woodwork on social media said that's me I'm this person and that became kind of a big deal at that at that point I said oh okay well then this kind of matches the lipid energy model which I talked you about before on prior podcast um we need to study it and of course this would be a great population to study risk in because we we just

chunks.title: Familial Hypercholesterolemia vs Lean Mass Hyperresponders
chunks.summary: The discussion differentiates between familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) and the lean mass hyperresponder phenotype. The segment clarifies common misconceptions and highlights the genetic versus phenotypic factors influencing LDL levels.
chunks.transcript: hypercholesterolemia and I should have said at the beginning of this podcast this one may be a little technical all of my podcasts with Dave tend to be but I'll try to make this more accessible for all of you guys listening so there is this this I would say genotype there is this familial hyper cholesterol familial hyper cholesterolemia genotype whereby people have a genetic reason over 200 different polymorphisms mutations they get high levels of LDL cholesterol so there's two points I just want to highlight there number one talk to us about why these two are not the same this is something you and I have talked about on podcasts in the past why familial hypercholesterolemia is not the same phys physiology as a lean mass hyperr or someone like myself who has an LDL of 160 um but is metabolically healthy and then is FH a phenotype or a genotype because I'm in sort of a standing disagreement with people over this I've heard people say oh FH is a phenotype and I say it's not a freaking it's a genotype it's a genetic mutation

chunks.title: Study Design and Purpose
chunks.summary: The duo elaborates on the design and purpose of their study, emphasizing its goal to isolate the effects of high LDL levels in metabolically healthy individuals. The conversation touches upon methodology, including the use of CT angiography for measuring plaque buildup.

chunks.title: Preliminary Findings of the Study
chunks.summary: Preliminary findings from the study are revealed, highlighting no statistically significant difference in plaque burden between lean mass hyperresponders and a control group, despite the former having significantly higher LDL levels.

chunks.title: Discussion on Lipid Hypothesis
chunks.summary: Feldman and his host discuss the mainstream lipid hypothesis and its implications regarding cardiovascular disease. They call for a reevaluation of the notion that higher LDL levels universally correlate with increased cardiac risk.

chunks.title: Reception and Future Directions
chunks.summary: The pair explore the reception of their findings within the medical community and discuss the potential future directions of lipid research. They emphasize the need for continued exploration of the relationship between LDL levels and cardiovascular health.

chunks.title: Conclusion and Call to Action
chunks.summary: The podcast concludes with a call for listeners to support further research and emphasizes the importance of understanding individual health contexts. Feldman shares how people can stay involved and informed about these developments.
